summary: My family and I moved to St. Paul, Minnesota, about eleven months ago in 2014. It was a big change for us because everything was new, including the cold weather. We came here to join my brother and sister and to have better opportunities, especially for education and taking care of my aging parents. Even though the weather is very different, we are happy to live here and appreciate the chances we have.

Before coming to the United States, my family lived in Burma, but we could not stay there because of the war. I was just a baby then, so I don't know all the details, but I heard that Burmese soldiers killed many Karen people, including my grandfather and uncle. Life was very hard because children couldn't go to school and families had to keep moving to survive. Due to this violence and instability, we had no choice but to leave Burma.

After leaving Burma, my family moved to Thailand, where I lived for many years and had the chance to go to school. Although Thailand is a poor country, I loved living there because I could study and eventually finish high school and college. I also learned some English while studying there. Thailand was where I developed many skills, including weaving and knitting my Karen clothes, and where I participated in church and cultural events like the Karen New Year.

During my college years in Thailand, I did a ten-month internship in Mae Sot, where I worked closely with my coordinator to provide training to people from Karen villages. This experience was important for me because it allowed me to help my community and gain practical skills. Mae Sot was a key place in my education and development as I prepared to graduate in 2012.

The Karen New Year is a very special event for me and my people. When I lived in Thailand, all Karen students had to attend the celebration, and if we didn't, we had to pay a fine. On this day, everyone wears traditional Karen clothes, which are colorful and beautifully woven by the women. It is a time of pride and cultural expression, and I always wore my Karen clothes to church and school, especially on Karen New Year Day.

KCFC is the church where I am very active in the youth group. I was honored when all the youth chose me to be the youth singer leader, which means I find songs for us to sing every Sunday. I love singing in church and practicing every Saturday at the youth pastor's house. KCFC has been an important part of my life, connecting me to my faith and community.

We came to the United States in 2014 to join my brother and sister and to have better opportunities, especially for education and to improve my English. Living here is very different from Thailand and Burma, especially because of the cold weather, but I love it because we have the freedom to do what we want. I also help take care of my elderly parents here, which is important since my siblings are busy working. Overall, living in the US has been a positive and hopeful experience for my family.
